title: practical english usage - Part 104
content:
the top of the page (     the-pageb top) not the roof of the house I        the house's roof) not the back of the room (     dwrmnn s buck) not the name of the street (     the street's name) common, and a structure with a preposition (usually of) is more normal.

With nouns which  are not the names of people, animals, countries, etc,  's is less 3  's not   used:   the    name       of   the    street the husband of the woman who sent you those papers my sister's husband Compare: An o/-strueture is preferred when the ‘ possessing’  expression is very long.

the committee's second meeting   the second meeting of the committee or the Queen's arrival   the arrival of the Queen With some words for people’ s actions, we  can use either ’ $ or a structure with of.

The government's decision was extremely unwise.

What are Norway's main exports?

I didn 't believe the girl's story. Have you read Jack's email?

We   also use the structure to talk about things that people, etc produce.

What do you think of the company's management?

Scotland's climate is getting warmer.

There's something wrong with the cat's ear.

Nouns     and   Noun    Phrases;     Agreement        Section  11 grammar *125  nouns in combination: milk chocolate a ticket office (= an office that sells tickets) a toothbrush (= a brush for teeth)                    coat pockets (= pockets in coats) a shoe shop (= a shop that sells shoes)             a horse race (= a race for horses) meaning. (For exceptions, ►     125.4.) Note that the first noun is usually singular in form, even if it has a plural 2 the   first    noun    is  singular:     a  shoe      shop the airport bus (the bus goes to the airport) a Birmingham man (the man comes from Birmingham) a sheepdog (the dog  looks after sheep) an oil well (the well produces oil) becomes a subject.

Noun + noun expressions are often related to structures where the second noun a flower garden (a kind of garden) - a garden flower (a kind of flower) a race horse (a kind of horse) - a horse race (a kind of race) chocolate milk (a kind of milk) - milk chocolate (a kind of chocolate) adjective. Compare: In this structure, the first noun modifies or describes the second, a little like an Many common ideas in English are expressed by noun + noun compounds.

1  milk     chocolate;          chocolate           milk 125  nouns     in  combination:        milk     chocolate Alice is at the dentist('s).

the butcher are often used without 's.

In modern English, expressions like the doctor, the dentist, the hairdresser, We had a nice time at Jack and Susan's last night.

I bought it at Smiths. She got married at St Joseph's.

way. The  apostrophe is often dropped in the names of shops and companies.

We   often talk about shops, companies, churches and people's houses in this 'Whose is that? 'Daniel's.' We   can use a possessive without a following noun, if the meaning is clear.

8  possessive        without      a  noun For the  's structure in compound nouns (e.g. a doll's house, cow's milk), ►     126.

a pound's worth of walnuts three dollars' worth of popcorn Note the use of the 's structure before worth.

yesterday's news last Sunday's match tomorrow's weather We   can also use the 's structure to talk about particular moments and events.

6 other     expressions         of  time:   yesterday's           news grammar •   125  nouns in combination: milk chocolate a sun hat (= a hat that protects you against the sun) army officers (= officers in the army) combinations.

Articles belonging to the first (modifying) noun are dropped in noun + noun 5 articles woman-haters (= people who  hate women) - man-eaters (= lions, tigers or other animals that eat people) women pilots (= women who  fly planes) - men drivers (= men who  drive) Compare: ‘ subject' meaning; man and woman are used to express an ‘ object' meaning.

We use the plurals men and women to modify plural nouns when they have a athletics training an economics degree Note also that singular nouns ending in -ics can be used before other nouns.
